title: Aspect in the past tense
subtitle: Choosing the right aspect for past events

vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Я купил хлеб. / Я покупаю хлеб каждый день.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: Ya kupil khleb. / Ya pokupayu khleb kazhdiy den'.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I bought bread (done). / I buy bread every day.

vocabulary.meaning: to come, arrive (impf.) / to come, arrive (pf., one time)
vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Он приходил каждый день. / Он пришёл в пять часов.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: On prikhodil kazhdiy den'. / On prishyol v pyat' chasov.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: He used to come every day. / He came (arrived) at five o'clock.

vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Сначала я поел, потом лёг спать.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: Snachala ya poel, potom lyog spat'.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: First I ate, then I went to bed.
First I was reading, then I was writing.
I waited for a long time.

hook.text: Past tense is where aspect choice is most noticeable. The difference between 'I was reading' and 'I read it' — or 'he came every day' and 'he arrived at five' — is aspect. Both use past tense forms; the aspect determines the meaning. This lesson gives you the full picture.

why.text: The Russian past tense is simpler than the present: verbs agree in gender and number, not person. But you still choose aspect. The choice depends on what you want to communicate: the ongoing activity (imperfective) or the completed result (perfective). Certain contexts demand one or the other — not a stylistic preference, but a grammatical requirement.

cards.title: Past tense forms — gender agreement
cards.body: Russian past tense agrees with the subject in gender and number, not person. Remove the infinitive ending (-ть or -ти) and add: -л (masculine), -ла (feminine), -ло (neuter), -ли (plural). This applies to both aspects.

examples.meaning: read and finished (m / f / pl) — perfective

cards.title: When to use imperfective past
cards.body: Use imperfective past when: (1) describing an ongoing activity at a point in time, (2) describing a habitual/repeated past action, (3) describing background activity while something else happened, (4) emphasising the process, not the result.

examples.meaning: Yesterday I was reading all day. (ongoing, all day)
He used to come every day. (habitual past)
I waited for a long time. (process, duration)

cards.title: When to use perfective past
cards.body: Use perfective past when: (1) describing a single completed event, (2) describing a sequence of events (each event completed before the next), (3) expressing a result that is relevant now, (4) with уже (already) and наконец (finally).

examples.meaning: He arrived at five. (single, completed event)

examples.cyrillic: Я купил хлеб и пошёл домой. (СВ + СВ)
examples.transliteration: Ya kupil khleb i poshyol domoy.
examples.meaning: I bought bread and went home. (sequence: done, then done)
He finally wrote the letter. (completed result)

questions.prompt: Yesterday he was reading all day: Вчера он ___ весь день.
questions.explanation: All day = duration of process → imperfective past. читал.

questions.prompt: She finally finished writing the letter: Наконец она ___ письмо.
questions.explanation: наконец + completed result → perfective. написала (feminine past of написать).
наконец (nakonets) = finally / at last. Triggers perfective aspect.

cards.title: Sequences of events — perfective
cards.body: When telling a story with a sequence of events that each happened one after the other, use perfective for each step. This is the 'narrative past' — a series of completed actions moving the story forward. Imperfective would describe background or process, not progression.

examples.cyrillic: Я встал, оделся и пошёл на работу.
examples.transliteration: Ya vstal, odelsa i poshyol na rabotu.
examples.meaning: I got up, got dressed, and went to work. (three completed steps, sequence)

examples.cyrillic: Она купила билет и вошла в автобус.
examples.transliteration: Ona kupila bilet i voshla v avtobus.
examples.meaning: She bought a ticket and got on the bus. (sequence of two completed actions)

cards.title: The 'annulled action' — imperfective
cards.body: Imperfective past can describe an action that happened but whose result no longer holds — sometimes called the 'annulled action'. Он открывал окно (he opened the window [but it's now closed again]). Perfective он открыл окно implies the result still stands.

examples.meaning: I took the book. (but gave it back)
I took the book. (I have it now)

questions.prompt: I bought bread (one time, done): Я ___ хлеб.

questions.explanation: долго (dolgo) = for a long time. Triggers imperfective (duration of process).

questions.prompt: He used to come every day: Раньше он ___ каждый день.
questions.explanation: Habitual past (каждый день / раньше) → imperfective. приходил.
наконец + single completed event → perfective. пришла (feminine past of прийти).

questions.prompt: I got up, got dressed, went to work — each verb is ___.
questions.explanation: Narrative sequence of completed events → each verb is perfective.

questions.prompt: I was waiting for a long time: Я долго ___.
questions.explanation: долго (for a long time, duration of process) → imperfective. ждал.

rules: Imperfective past: process, duration, habit, background — 'was doing', 'used to do'
Perfective past: single completed event, sequence, result — 'did and finished'
Sequence of events (narrative): each step = perfective
Context clues: долго/всегда/раньше → imperfective; уже/наконец/вдруг → perfective
summary.nextLesson: Next: aspect in the future tense — and choosing aspect for commands (imperative).

questions.prompt: Yesterday she was reading all day: Вчера она ___ весь день.
Past tense agreement: a female speaker saying 'I read (finished)' uses:
I bought bread and went home — both verbs are:
He used to come every day: Он ___ каждый день.
I waited for a long time: Я долго ___.
